data.title: TD Synnex: I See More Upside As Hyve Continues To Deliver
data.description:
Thesis

TD SYNNEX Corporation (SNX) is basically a middleman for the tech world, but a really important one. Companies that build the digital backbone (laptops, servers, software, cloud systems) don’t typically deal directly with every business or corner shop. That’s a needlessly sloppy and inefficient way to go about it. Instead, they rely on companies like SNX to handle that whole process. It purchases large quantities of technology products and services, then sells them to many smaller businesses, such as IT consultants, resellers, and managed service providers. Those smaller businesses then sell, set up, or support these products and services for the final customers. It’s not complicated once you see it. They basically run on margin plus services.

It's been a while since I last covered SNX with a Buy based on its undervaluation, steady earnings growth, and exposure to higher-growth areas like cloud and data infrastructure. Since then, shares have delivered an exceptional total return, comfortably outperforming the S&P 500 (SP500) by a wide margin.

Fresh off Q1 2026 earnings and a predictably upbeat market response, the stock is back on its upward track. And after going over the latest data, I’m still leaning bullish, as the core business remains resilient and the growth drivers behind its recent momentum are still firmly in place.

Breakdown of TD SYNNEX’s Q1 2026 Earnings

  • Distribution gross billings growth: +17% YoY
  • Hyve gross billings growth: +95% YoY.

My first read is that the quarter’s strength came from two very different parts of the business both doing well simultaneously. Looking at the distribution side of things, company’s management said demand was strong across multiple areas: infrastructure, security, software, PCs, and even networking, which had been weak before but is now starting to recover. In Hyve, I wasn't surprised to see that the main reason for growth was strong demand from hyperscaler customers, it's all about the ongoing AI buildout. And most of this growth's still coming from the biggest customers, although new projects are expected to start picking up later in the year. It helps explain why revenue, margins, and profitability all rose together.

  • Revenue: $17.16B (+18% YoY)
  • Non-GAAP EPS: $4.73 (+69% YoY)
  • Non-GAAP operating income growth: +48% YoY.

On the distribution side, the main issue was pricing. Management says rising memory and component costs helped justify higher average selling prices. This also caused some customers to buy earlier than planned. However, they emphasized that these effects were noticeable but not very large or extreme.

  • ASP / pull-forward impact: ~2 pts of growth.

I think that makes sense. The latest industry data does back up their point. For example, TrendForce said this week that contract prices for DRAM and NAND memory are rising quite quickly. This is because more of the available supply is being used for servers and enterprise SSDs, leaving less supply for the rest of the market and making overall availability tighter. IDC has also said that the memory shortage is now increasing total PC revenues, even though fewer units are being sold. This matches what management is saying, that higher ASPs (average selling prices) can make up for some drop in demand, especially in commercial PCs more than in consumer PCs.

  • Distribution operating margin: 2.0% (+34 bps YoY).

That helps explain why their profit margins actually increased. More or less, what the management team's really saying is that they made smart decisions. They bought their products at the right times, stayed in close and careful contact with both their suppliers and their customers, and used their inventory prudently so they could absorb price changes instead of scrambling after the fact.

I think that makes sense in the short term. But I wouldn’t get carried away and assume this is the new normal. Larger distributors, with strong supplier connections and buying power, can take advantage of supply disruptions better than smaller competitors. Even so, this doesn’t necessarily mean their higher profits will last long-term.

Meanwhile, customers were doing a bit better than expected. That said, there were still some signs things could be unstable. Orders waiting to be filled are increasing, there are lots of price quotes being requested, and customers are placing orders earlier than usual because prices don’t stay valid for long and some products are still hard to get. So this tends to give the company a clearer short-term view of demand, but it doesn’t remove the risk. Customers may just be picking their spots more carefully on timing and purchases and management did flag that demand could fall off in the second half.

  • Hyve operating income growth: +66% YoY
  • Hyve operating margin: 4.2%.

Hyve's definitely the more interesting piece strategically. Its revenue mix and margins are shifting with AI infrastructure. But management was fairly clear that the recent spike is still mostly coming from just two big customers. And you can’t ignore that concentration risk. At the same time, they are landing new hyperscale programs and moving into more complete rack- and system-level builds. I think that should make them more relevant to customers and, over time, probably help their positioning.

On balance, the broader setup does seem to support it. In mid-March, DIGITIMES reported that hyperscalers are increasing their spending, which is boosting demand across the entire AI server supply chain. This includes parts like interconnects and circuit boards. In other words, it’s not just them, it’s a wider trend hitting the whole industry.

  • Free cash flow (Q1): $(929)M
  • Cash: $1.6B
  • Leverage: 1.5x.

Cash flow was the only part that didn’t look as strong as the income statement at first glance, but there’s a clear reason for it. The company used up cash in its day-to-day operations because it deliberately increased inventory in its distribution network and kept investing money to support Hyve’s growth. A clear positive is the strong cash position and relatively reasonable debt levels. And this puts them in a good position to keep investing in the business while also returning money to shareholders.

Above, you can see for yourselves SNX's superior dividend growth rate over the broader market (SPY).

Overall, Europe's still holding up well rather than getting worse. According to IDC, IT spending in Europe, the Middle East, and Africa is expected to grow this year, mainly driven by software. Businesses and service providers are still spending at a healthy level, even though consumer spending remains weak. Given that environment, management’s aiming to grow faster than the overall market in Europe, focusing on helping medium and large businesses upgrade and modernize their systems, and being careful with acquisitions, that is, targeting deals with solid returns instead of just getting bigger for the sake of it.

SNX’s Valuation and Risk Profile

I think it’s fair to say that SNX still appears a bit undervalued when you look at its earnings multiple, but not by a large amount. The stock's trading below the fair value range suggested by the FAST Graphs analysis, and its current valuation is also clearly lower than the typical levels seen across the sector, both based on past earnings and expected future earnings.

That lower valuation makes sense because this is a distribution business, which typically gets lower valuation multiples. It’s also more sensitive to economic cycles, has naturally thinner profit margins, and strong earnings tend to trigger questions about how long they’ll hold up.

That said, the outlook starts to raise some flags. Expected revenue growth of only 5.4% and long-term earnings per share growth of around 10% are not especially strong. Their healthy balance sheet and better management of working capital give them some cushion to handle challenges. But if growth slows down, the current lower valuation may end up being justified rather than a bargain.

Final Takeaway

All things considered, I think SNX's still a good Buy. There’s room for gains if Hyve continues to grow steadily and keeps its profit margins in distribution stable. Meanwhile, balance sheet strength and valuation provide some downside support. It fits for investors seeking a reasonably priced compounder with AI infrastructure exposure who can accept cyclicality.

data.title: Simon Property Group: Implications On The Passing Of The CEO
data.description:
Simon Property Group (SPG) announced the passing of David Simon, CEO (Chairman Of The Board and President as well), who was really the single most important person in the company ever since it went public. Along with the announcement came a declaration that his son Eli Simon would become the new CEO with an independent non-executive chairman.

Rarely does any company replace someone like David Simon with another person at the same skill level. David Simon grew Simon property group into the investment grade company that it is today with probably one of the best diversification strategies in the business.

Because the primary advantage of a business (like the mall business that is the main concern here) is location, there is very unlikely to be a noticeable change at first. Instead, business will likely continue as usual for a while.

This is especially true as the company had long emphasized that there was a transition program in place just in case it was needed. So, there is every possibility that the son will be very capable to continue the business in adequate fashion.

All About Locations

The last article noted the latest dividend increase. Also noted in line with this was the conservative financial profile.

But now with the change in senior officers (and possibly a few more likely to be announced), what may make a difference in the future is location choices. There may also be a change in locations "that go downhill". These deteriorating situations need to be handled promptly, or it could at some point affect future growth.

But these things will be noticed over the years rather than a big immediate change from my last article. There is certainly no rush to make any investment decisions now.

Many of the decisions made in the past will likely last several years. That means the last article is unlikely to become obsolete any time soon.

The other thing to consider is that Simon Property Group is much larger now than it was when it first went public a few decades back. That means that future growth will be inevitably slower than it was just due to the size considerations.

So, what is considered a good job by top management has to be reframed to take into account the current company. Comparing the job performance of the new CEO to past accomplishments could well be an irrelevant comparison (and unfair as well). It is really about what superior management looks like going forward with a much larger company.

Stock Price

But if the current advantages are likely to last for a while, then the stock price performance should follow suit.

The stock price is up about one-third from its last high point until it reached $200 (give or take) recently. To me that means the growing dividend and the appreciation of the stock over time should probably continue to average about 10% per year.

Now if that slows materially, then it is time to evaluate the new management. Any growth story has a limit that is usually due to the skills of senior management. Simon, as one of the largest REIT's in the industry is likely to reach that upper limit sooner than many competitors.

However, this is a highly fractured industry. Therefore, there should be plenty of room for that slower growth mentioned earlier.

The current Quant System rating on the Seeking Alpha Website has the stock listed as a hold. Normally with the change in the CEO position, I would agree. However, the real estate business is largely a "location business". Either the company has a location advantage, or it does not. This company has that advantage.

Therefore, my best guess is that previous decisions have conferred an advantage that should show itself as continued industry outperformance for the next couple of years.

First Quarter

But that means that the first quarter should continue the long very good history that David Simon established before his passing. In fact, that very good record should continue for the rest of the fiscal year and probably the next year. To me that means a likely total return in the low teens for the foreseeable future unless something goes very wrong.

Now, there could be some inflation that may affect interest rates the company pays on its debt in the future. But the investment grade debt rating indicates that this company should be able to handle whatever challenges appear (financially).

The other consideration is that the malls themselves are likely to make up for inflation by charging higher rates. This might eventually overcome an initial interest rate boost (that might result in lower earnings initially). Inflation is generally seen as a long-term friend of real estate.

Summary

Rather than this be a hold as the quant system shows, I look at this as a long-term strong buy consideration. The timing as to when to get in may be currently uncertain due to the Iranian crisis. But to me, that is an execution decision as opposed to whether to rate this as a strong buy or not.

Simon Property Group has long been a leader in the REIT industry. I see that continuing for some time to come, despite the death of the CEO.

We will have to watch the new CEO establish an independent track record going forward. But that process takes place with the business currently having a big location and diversification advantage. Those advantages will not disappear "overnight".

Instead, there will be a gradual process of evaluating the long-term performance of the REIT as locations gradually change in value as time moves on. There should be a process set up whereby subpar locations are periodically eliminated (one way or another) while superior locations are added. For a company of the size of Simon, it will likely take time for these future decisions to become significant.

But individual investors and institutions can "grade" each decision as it is made so that an investment strategy can be formed as needed before there is too much backsliding (if that occurs).

No one is worried about the possibility that the new CEO is equally or more talented than the CEO that just passed away because that possibility does not involve a loss of principal.

Risks

The greatest risk appears to be a slowing economy combined with the initial effects of inflation. A slowing economy decreases the needs for malls (and shopping in general). Meanwhile the inflation threat increases the cost of debt as interest rates rise.

But in the long-run, no matter the situation, this company has outperformed the industry due to its diversification and location advantages. That outperformance is highly likely to continue.

Even if the stock were to drop as it did back in fiscal year 2020, the superior management strategy allowed the stock to recover all losses while a recent acquisition shows the growth strategy remains in place. Growing the business allows the market to see a pathway to a higher stock value in the future.

I personally tend to hold something like this until either stock gets way overvalued or until I see a change in management execution (for the worse).

data.title: John B. Sanfilippo & Son: This Family Business Is Nuts
data.description:
Expansion Leads To Temporarily Low Free Cash Flow

Small cap stocks can be harder to analyze because individual events like changing purchase patterns at a major customer or large capital projects can have a big temporary impact on financials. However, companies that have repeatedly worked through similar issues in the past often deserve the benefit of the doubt when predicting future performance. That appears to be the case with John B. Sanfilippo & Son (JBSS), a $900 million market cap distributor of nuts, mixes, and snack bars. I have covered JBSS previously on Seeking Alpha from 2020-2022. While I sold the stock in December 2022, it was more to raise cash for personal needs than any out of any concern about the stock. Nevertheless, the stock has not done much since then, still trading below where it was at that time and producing a total return of around 2.1% annualized.

2025 was a particularly tough year for the stock as the price declined along with the total dividend payout, but the lower dividends were for a good reason. JBSS has been reinvesting in its own growth by expanding into the faster-growing snack and nutrition bar market. The company bought a bar manufacturing facility from TreeHouse Foods in fiscal 2024 and increased capital spending in fiscal 2025 and 2026 to further expand bar production capacity. (The company's fiscal year ends in June.)

The temporary reduction in free cash flow could be concerning if only looking at the numbers, but JBSS has come through other periods of high spending in good shape and with continued growth. The company also manages the balance sheet conservatively, avoiding long-term debt except for secured loans such as mortgages, capital leases, or equipment-backed loans. This causes some fluctuation in the special dividends paid to shareholders but avoids excessive leverage and interest expense. To see how this strategy has worked out for the company, a quick review of history is useful.

Welcome To The Family

Despite the current name, JBSS was founded by John B. Sanfilippo and his father, Gaspare, in downtown Chicago in 1922. The business was originally engaged in shelling pecans. After their only customer went out of business in the 1930s, the company diversified into other types of nuts. Over the years, JBSS grew and acquired its own retail brands and distribution while continuing to expand its capabilities to package nuts for third party customers. JBSS also vertically integrated by establishing processing facilities near key nut growing areas. The company progressively moved its headquarters into larger and larger facilities, ending up in the western suburb of Elgin, IL around 2006, just as John's grandsons Jeffrey (CEO) and Jasper (COO) became the fourth generation of Sanfilippos to run the company.

This new headquarters was financed by mortgage debt, and while JBSS continued to do well, the stock was beaten up during the financial crisis, creating many opportunities between 2006 and 2012 to buy below the $12 level where the company came public in 1991.

JBSS resumed its growth in the 2010s, buying several nut and snack mix brands including Orchard Valley Harvest before the latest expansion into snack and nutrition bars.

While this history is interesting on its own, it also shows that JBSS has been successful at deleveraging after a big expansion while delivering volume and profit growth. The mortgage on the Elgin facility is now paid off, with the only long-term debt now consisting of a mortgage on the Selma, TX peanut processing facility and an equipment loan secured by the new bar manufacturing line.

The new capacity is scheduled to be up and running in July 2026, which is the start of Fiscal 2027. With less need for capex once the new line is installed, I expect free cash flow to get back to pre-2024 levels, enabling the equipment loan to be paid off while still allowing dividends to increase.

There are a couple other quirks about JBSS new investors should be aware of. First, it truly is a family business, with the Sanfilippo family holding special Class A shares which have the same economic interest as common shares but 10 times the voting power. Overall, the family has about 75% of the total voting power. They also have the right to elect seven board members, currently composed of Jeffrey, Jasper, and other siblings and cousins. Common shareholders have the right to elect three independent board members. While this close control can be a risk, the family has treated common shareholders well, paying a generous though variable dividend over time, and avoiding excessive compensation. JBSS has not done any buybacks, yet the share count has only grown from 11.2 million to 11.7 million since June 2016 due to share based compensation, a growth rate of just 0.5% per year.

As hinted at earlier, the dividend policy is unusual. JBSS has paid a regular dividend only after the end of each fiscal year since 2017. This payout has increased $0.05 each year and reached $0.90 in 2025. In addition, the company often pays special dividends. This often happens at the same time as the annual regular dividend but also can occur at any time.

Despite the variability, since 2015, the overall dividend yield had been around 4%, although it has been higher in years like 2019-2021 when it was helped first by lower raw nut prices, then by pandemic-induced home snacking and baking demand. That earlier spike made the dropoff in 2024 and 2025 look concerning, but the company was investing the cash in long-term growth with the TreeHouse acquisition and bar production capex.

Capex is again expected to be high in 2026 as JBSS wraps up the bar manufacturing project. Total capex for the year was forecasted to be $104 million, of which $47.3 million was actually spent in the first half. Still, the company appears confident in cash generation, with a $1.50 per share special dividend recently declared. This dividend is payable on May 21 to shareholders who buy the stock by April 24.

Current Challenges And Opportunities

JBSS has improved its margins over the past 10 years by selling a higher percentage of total sales into the consumer channel, which made up 82% of sales in fiscal 2025 compared to 62% in 2015. (Commercial Ingredients and Contract Packaging make up the other two channels.) However, within the consumer channel, JBSS has faced some challenges with its own brands including Fisher and Orchard Valley Harvest, where distribution can sometimes be at the mercy of large retailers. Walmart (WMT) made up 40% of JBSS sales in 2025 and Target (TGT) was another 11%. The company has increased private label sales which include store brands less likely to be displaced by other name brands. The private label business now makes up 83% of consumer channel sales.

Like many consumer products, nuts and bars enjoyed a demand spike during the pandemic for home snacking and baking, followed by a decline in subsequent years. As we see in the company's last shareholder presentation, volumes declined from 2022 to 2024 but appear to have bottomed. Overall category 2025 volumes are above 2024 in both nuts and bars. Nut volumes remain below 2021 levels, however, while bar volumes are higher. This supports the current company strategy to diversify into bars.

Valuation

JBSS has seen steady sales growth since 2021 and net income growth since 2019, but the share price is still trading at 2018 levels.

As a result, the stock's price/sales ratio of 0.8 is similar to where it was in the 2016-2018 time frame. The price/earnings ratio of 13.2, while above 2025 levels, is still cheaper than at any point between 2016 and 2024. These values compare favorably to the Consumer Staples sector averages, which currently are 1.0 on P/S and 19.8 on P/E (both on a trailing 12-month basis).

JBSS currently has just one professional analyst following the stock and issuing earnings estimates. In January, he lowered the FY 2026 earnings per share estimate from $5.94 to $5.57 but raised FY 2027 from $7.14 to $7.31.

The estimate cut for FY 2026 gave JBSS a D+ grade on Earnings Revisions in the Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings, and is a caution flag considering the fiscal H1 actual EPS of $3.12, implying earnings of just $2.45 for the second half. However, the recent special dividend declaration suggests greater company confidence in current conditions. Also, the EPS growth of 31% for FY 2027 is impressive for a packaged food stock and suggests a permanent boost in earnings power from the bar investment. The price target of $109 also still offers upside from current levels.

Quant Ratings also give JBSS a D+ on Valuation, however that could be driven by a low dividend yield that does not reflect the recent special dividend. As the recent special and year-end dividends coming in August get incorporated into the calculation, the Valuation score could improve along with the EPS Revision score, causing JBSS's Quant Score to improve considerably from the current 2.85 in the Hold range.

Conclusion

Compared to the May-October 2025 period when JBSS was trading in the $60-$70 range, it may be a bit later than optimal to call it a Buy. Nevertheless, the improved cash flow driven by the completion of the investment in snack bar capacity is still on the way. Earnings are also set to take a step up in fiscal 2027 as the bar production comes online. This would leave JBSS attractively valued even in the low $80s, still with 30% upside based on the Wall Street price target.

Appendix: Trading Strategy

As a small cap stock, JBSS can trade with low liquidity. The recent special dividend announcement could be causing the price to spike in the short term. I recommend using limit orders and bidding for no more than a couple hundred shares at a time. Alternatively, investors could sell cash-secured puts to buy the stock if it falls below the strike price. Just be aware of wide bid-ask spreads and small position sizes on the puts as well. Also, note that strike prices may be adjusted in the event of a special dividend. For example, I recently reacquired the stock at an effective price of $76.36 by selling puts with a strike price of $78.40. These were originally $80 puts, adjusted for special dividends of $0.60 in August 2025 and $1.00 in December 2025.

data.title: Intel: From Underdog To AI CPU Comeback Leader
data.description:
From Underdog To A Leader

I turned bullish on Intel Corporation (INTC) stock back in January 2025, when most investors and analysts were skeptical about the firm's prospects. I should say they were doubtful for a very good reason: Intel has managed to lose its moat to rivals, specifically Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), and there was little hope that years of unprofitable foundry business would stop, and the earnings would reverse to the positive side. But I saw some tailwinds on the horizon, and my bullish thesis since then has fully played out as INTC has managed to gain over 150% - my call for the prioritization of longer-term thinking worked out.

I believe that INTC is still a Buy because we haven't even seen the monetization of the AI-driven CPU demand - everyone was talking about GPUs throughout the past 5-7 years, but CPUs are currently gaining traction with the LLMs shifting from training to inference and orchestration that are heavily CPU-intensive in nature. We've already seen price hikes from the company, based on some sources, which is going to let INTC reverse earnings at a faster-than-initially-anticipated pace, in my view. Also, I like the ongoing cost-cutting and key management targets for the next quarters - Intel should eventually fix its operating leverage story, and if so, the current valuation doesn't look as stretched as it might seem at first glance. Based on all this, I'm reiterating my Buy rating on INTC.

Why Do I Think So?

The stock hasn't moved much since the publication of the Q4 2025 results in late January. Intel had $13.67 billion in sales (-4.1% YoY) and $0.15 in adjusted EPS for the quarter, beating both headline consensus figures, but then most analysts decided to downgrade their estimates for the upcoming Q1 report, according to Seeking Alpha data.

The market isn't cheering the short-term results from Intel Corporation, which is logical, as there's nothing to cheer about just yet. The stock is growing amid the market's repricing of Intel's better execution (with the new management team), the prospects of the commercial deployment of the highly anticipated Intel 18A process node, and now the rising role of CPUs, where Intel still holds a massive market share (although it's been falling for years).

Before discussing the CPU opportunity, let me start with what's happening internally. I see much better cost control since the time the new management stepped in. For example, the new CEO, Lip-Bu Tan, reduced management layers to accelerate decision-making and then instilled strict capital expenditure discipline that prioritizes only the main growth-driving products. As a result, in FY2025, we've seen a 15% lower adjusted OPEX on a YoY basis. For the current fiscal year (FY2026), they're targeting $16 billion in OPEX, so it's going to be another ~3% cut. On the CAPEX side, specifically in Q4, Intel strictly managed the expenditures at only $4 billion, while also achieving a positive adjusted free cash flow of $2.2 billion (the Q4 CFO came in at $4.3 billion for the quarter). I also liked that on the gross margin level, Intel has managed to beat its own guidance, having delivered ~37.9% in Q4, which was 140 bps above guidance. The cost control is clearly better at the firm now, in my assessment.

Now, let's jump to Intel's CPU opportunity. The new phase of LLMs' (large language models) development - inference and orchestration - requires not just GPUs, but also CPUs, in large quantities. A lot of people refer to "agentic AI," which is about autonomous software agents making decisions and interacting with specific tools to perform complex tasks - this is where we get to the CPUs' territory, as some analysts say. CPUs run the systems while GPUs run the models - the more calls the AI agents make to various tools/databases/apps, the more the primary bottleneck shifts away from raw compute toward network and overall system orchestration. We've already gotten a clear confirmation that proves this point - according to Massed Compute, the CPU-to-GPU ratio in AI servers has recently risen from 1:12 to 1:8, with hyperscalers now targeting a ratio of 1:4.

All this means to me that the share of CPU usage is increasing quite rapidly right now, and so it should be a major bullish sign for Intel. Most enterprises seem to understand this heating trend. According to Jukan's research, CSPs (Chinese Cloud Service Providers) like Alibaba (BABA) and Tencent (TCEHY) have already increased their CPU orders by 50% since the beginning of this year. Reportedly, Intel's production line utilization has reached 95%, but is not yet physically at full capacity - all this amid Intel carrying out 2 rounds of CPU price hikes since the start of 2026, according to Invezz data. All this sounds extremely bullish for what might be coming for INTC's Q1 earnings update in 3 weeks from now, especially so when we recall the abundance of negative revisions from analysts following the Q4 release.

I believe Intel is very likely to beat on the top line following the price hikes that weren't visible 6 months ago (today's consensus is 1.29% lower than it was half a year ago, so the comparison base has been set lower).

Plus, if we assume that AI agents are only gaining traction in the next 3-5 years, as Nvidia's (NVDA) CEO and many other AI influencers foresee, the cuts to Intel's 2028 top-line expansion that we see above are going to reverse strongly. Otherwise, INTC will be easily beating them quarter after quarter.

The company's management is clearly treating this new AI trend as something significant - the latest news that the company is buying the 49% equity interest in its Fab 34 facility in Ireland from Apollo for $14.2 billion is yet another confirmation of this assumption. Also, it's going to be accretive to the bottom line, so again, it makes me almost certain that we shall see upgrades to the guidance when INTC reports for its Q1 results in late March.

The transaction is expected to be accretive to ongoing EPS while strengthening Intel's credit profile in 2027 and beyond. Intel continues to expect it will retire debt maturities as they come due in 2026 and 2027, according to the company.

Fab 34 is a high-volume semiconductor fabrication facility for products using the Intel 4 and Intel 3 process technologies, including Intel Core Ultra and Intel Xeon 6 processors.

Source: Seeking Alpha News.

Now, let's talk about INTC's valuation. A lot of analysts don't get this right, in my opinion, because they're comparing INTC with its past, with its worst-positioned peers, or they're trying to run a classical DCF model on future cash flows that are hard to envision just yet. Here are the findings of Argus Research's analysts on INTC's fair value:

I believe the stronger pricing and Intel's 2026 server CPU roadmap (Clearwater Forest and Diamond Rapids) should convince analysts that the firm is capable of showing much more than just $0.99/share in earnings for FY2027. I agree more with the higher end of the analysts' consensus range, which shows $1.80 in EPS for the fiscal year 2027:

At 30x earnings - a multiple that might be fair for Intel, given its role in the industry, and the post-2027 growth expectations - the fair value per share arrives at $54 if my above EPS input is right. In this case, the upside potential would be close to 12.5%, and it might be higher if the forward P/E of 40-50x is retained.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

Execution is the main risk to my bullish thesis. A lot of things should align in the next few years to let Intel efficiently capture the rising AI agentic demand for CPUs. Also, AMD isn't sleeping on this opportunity - Intel's constant loss of market share in the CPU space may continue, and we might see the stock drop like a rock if today's consensus estimates aren't met.

Valuation is another area that makes my Buy on Intel sound risky. UBS analysts updated their Neutral rating on the stock as they saw limited growth potential ahead (even though they raised their PT to $52/share from $49/share previously).

The consensus PT is pretty close to the current price as well - there's even a slight downside potential following Intel's latest rally:

The Bottom Line

While I agree that INTC is a highly risky turnaround story right now, I like the surrounding industry conditions that promise a strong uptick in orders at solid pricing - all this increases the odds for a successful recovery, in my opinion. The undervaluation I've calculated above might not look sufficient for some investors, especially as I'm taking the high-end of the consensus range for the FY2027 EPS input. But still, the actual EPS is hard to calculate right now, following the latest news that Intel has already raised selling prices for some of its products. We shall see how INTC reports in 3 weeks, but I'm expecting a beat on the bottom line, plus a very positive guidance from the management team. With all that said, I keep my Buy on INTC in place for now.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Why Does Bad News Sell Better Than Good News?
data.description:
The reason that the nightly news, which turned into a 24-hour news cycle, has always been focused on deaths, arrests, natural disasters, conflicts and any other development of a disconcerting nature. Bad news sells. People want to hear about it. David Muir, who is the anchor of ABC World News Tonight, started the novel idea of finishing his show with a happy story, no matter how trivial. It was his effort to cut through the constant onslaught of negativity and has become his signature modus operandi. The financial markets operate in a similar fashion, as investors always seem to have an ear for the skeptics, critics, and permabears whose voices become louder when markets are falling.

The problem pessimists have is that the stock market goes up most of the time. Yes, we have had a correction of 10% or more on average about once every 18 months since 1945. We have had a bear market decline of 20% or more on average every 4.5 years in data going back to 1929, although they have occurred less frequently over the past 50 years with one every seven years since 1970. In between, there seems to be a never ending competition to call the next top and subsequent decline in the market. Yet pundits who have been calling for that top repeatedly over the past one, two, or three-year period have lost their credibility.

Focusing on the negatives may draw more attention than a more balanced approach, but the reality is that no one calls the top at the top unless it’s the first time they called it and did so correctly. I will never try. I focus on leaning offensive or defensive in a diversified portfolio strategy that always maintains exposure to each asset class. I have been on offense since 2022, but there are now plenty of reasons to lean more defensively after the month-long war in Iran. President Trump’s speech last night was the most recent, and markets are expressing their disapproval with WTI crude oil soaring more than $8 to $108/barrel, while S&P 500 futures plunged 100 points from yesterday’s close. Aside from listing the military accomplishments over the past month, there was a deafening lack of details as to how the conflict would end, and what would be left behind. There was also an overly optimistic assumption that the Strait of Hormuz would open “naturally” when we left, and that oil and gasoline prices would fall abruptly and sharply thereafter. Markets are not buying what the President is selling. I suspect today’s market reaction will lead to more detail.

The most important question to ask is whether ending our military activities relatively soon will also end the U.S. economic expansion. If it does not, it will probably not end the bull market, which means that the recent correction is a selective buying opportunity in certain sectors and stocks. Elevated energy prices and disruptions in the global supply chain will adversely impact revenues and profits for certain sectors and companies, and it will slow the rate of economic growth. A deeper and more prolonged drawdown in risk asset prices would also be a huge factor. Yet recent economic data is still holding firm.

The ADP payroll report indicates we added 62,000 private sector jobs last month, which is better than expected. The main source of job growth, which is less encouraging, was healthcare and education, but we did see jobs added in construction, mining, finance, as well as leisure and hospitality.

The ISM Manufacturing Index rose to 52.7 in March, which was its third consecutive month in expansion, reflecting the strongest rate of growth since August 2022. The concern here remains that the prices paid sub-index rose to its highest level since June 2022, due to the increase in energy and other material input prices.

Retail sales in February rose a better than expected 0.6% and 3.7% over the prior year for the best performance in seven months, but this was obviously just before the war started. The gains were broadly based across all categories. Therefore, the primary engine of our economic growth was running fairly strong in advance of March’s mayhem.

The rally of the past two days had a lot to do with very oversold market conditions. The percentage of stocks in the S&P 500 trading above their 50-day moving average fell below 20% for the first time since last April. Prior declines were relatively short lived and followed by recovery rallies in the months that followed.

In fact, the average return for the S&P 500 over the following 12 months after falling within this zone has been 13.5%.

This seems unthinkable today, given the gravity of the circumstances, but remember that President Trump responds to markets more so than anything else. He views them as his report card, and after last night’s speech they are giving him an F. I expect to see him pivot again in short order and with more force in response to deteriorating financial market conditions. We may be winning the war on the military front, but Iran is winning it on the economic front so long as oil prices keep rising and markets keep falling.

data.title: Aehr Test Systems: The Story Matters More Than The Numbers
data.description:
Fundamentally, Aehr Test Systems (AEHR) looks ridiculously overvalued. The midpoint of guidance for the second half of fiscal 2026 (ending May) implies full-year revenue of $48.4 million. That figure would imply an 18% year-over-year decline from fiscal 2025 — in which revenue fell nearly 11%.

And yet, AEHR stock trades at 26.5x that revenue (adding back ~$30 million in cash on the balance sheet). It's a multiple that is one of the highest in the market (enterprise value to gross profit is in the range of 75x), and one that is nearing Aehr's recent highs:

Obviously, the market is trying to skate to where the puck is going. And, so far this fiscal year, Aehr has in fact instilled quite a bit of confidence among investors despite soft financial results. The generative artificial intelligence / data center boom positions Aehr's burn-in systems for potentially impressive growth in the near future. And with management expressing significant optimism on recent calls, and bookings expected to sharply outpace revenue, it does make some sense that the market is taking the long-term view.

But at the same time, as the chart above shows, we've been here before. It was the projected boom in electric vehicles that led AEHR to its highs early this decade. That story eventually fizzled out, AEHR fell nearly 80%, and it took the AI boom for the bull case to be resurrected.

With AEHR soaring into fiscal Q3 earnings next week — including a 23% gain Tuesday on the back of a big win in the data center market — expectations clearly are moving higher. It's up to Aehr and chief executive officer Gayn Erickson to keep those expectations up, and to keep the momentum in AEHR intact.

The Shifting Bull Case For AEHR Stock

The AEHR story has now shifted completely from EVs to AI. EV weakness is responsible for the soft results so far this year: incredibly, revenue from contactors (consumables used in the burn-in process) is down 71% year-over-year in the first half of FY26, which the 10-Q attributes to weakness in the EV market. At the same time, systems revenues have more than tripled (admittedly off a small base), largely due to growing demand from AI more broadly.

Erickson has said that Aehr isn't abandoning its legacy EV business (mostly in silicon carbide, or SiC). But investors essentially have. The focus has moved completely to AI, and it's been that market that has driven AEHR stock up 404% over the past twelve months.

To be fair, there's some logic to the market's shift in focus. Ai represents a massive opportunity for Aehr. As Erickson put it on the Q2 call, "So can the AI business be measured in hundreds of millions of dollars for Aehr Test a few years out? Yes. for sure." Against the current expectations for under $50 million in sales this year, that is obviously a massive opportunity.

Meanwhile, Aehr touches so many potential end markets. Aehr already has a lead customer in AI processor, and more on the way. The big win this week was in silicon photonics transceivers (that customer appears to be Cisco (CSCO), though it could also be Coherent (COHR)). Memory is a big opportunity; so is GaN (gallium nitride). A major hard disk drive supplier is adding new wafer-level FOX-CP systems.

And in those markets, Aehr can offer the wafer-level FOX, or the package-level Sonoma (acquired through the purchase of Incal Technology in 2024, which increasingly looks like an absolutely brilliant deal). Erickson himself seems absolutely convinced that wafer-level is the way to go, providing the most efficient way to weed out failures. (As he put it after Q1, wafer-level allows chipmakers to "shift left" in the process, catching failures as early as possible.)

So while revenue is low and the multiple currently high, there is a strong case for substantial growth over the next few years.

Aehr Needs More In Q3

That growth is unlikely to show up in revenue in the report next Tuesday. Indeed, many of the recent orders aren't likely to hit the P&L until fiscal 2027. But investors will be looking for good news in terms of bookings and wins.

After Q2, Aehr guided for second-half bookings in the range of $60 million to $80 million. Given the rally since (AEHR is up 74% since the Q2 release), that figure almost certainly needs to move to the higher end of the range.

And on a more granular level, investors will be looking for progress with qualifications in some of the major markets. After Q2, Erickson cited "meaningful progress" with benchmark evaluations from AI processors. Those evaluations typically take six months, and so won't be completed by Q3, but investors will be listening closely for more signs that new customers are on the way. The company's concentration of revenue with ON Semiconductor (ON) long has been a concern, and diversifying the revenue base is likely a necessary step toward driving the stock higher.

Flash memory is another potential source of news. Another benchmark evaluation in that end market was completed in December; it's notable at this point that an order hasn't yet been announced. (Erickson suggested after Q2 that Aehr would get a response in "the next couple months or so".)

But the near-term risk to earnings is that Aehr simply doesn't have anything left to add to the story with the release. Again, the numbers aren't going to cooperate. The major orders have been announced (and clearly been incorporated into the AEHR stock price). As good as the story is, valuation now clearly is a concern: AEHR now trades 9% above the consensus 12-month price target. There is room for analysts to update those targets, but Aehr needs to provide some new information to allow them to do so.

What Earnings Mean Longer-Term

As strong as AEHR has been over the past year, skeptics remain:

And as solid as the story has been of late, that skepticism too has basis in reality. Even Erickson admitted after Q1 that "I understand people are sort of in a doubting mode. Let us prove it to them."

At its core, the bull/bear debate over AEHR stock comes down to whether wafer-level burn-in and test actually is the most efficient method for chipmakers. Erickson clearly is a devout believer of the technology's potential across multiple end markets. But as I noted last year, WLTBI is not a new technology, and Aehr is not a new company. The company has been public for 29 years, and yet revenue still sits below $50 million.

The problem is that Erickson's belief in WLTBI simply isn't shared by the industry, at least so far. WLTBI adds an extra step, and many chipmakers don't see the value in taking the step. In SiC, for instance, ON has been the outlier; investors for years have waited for a second major customer in that business. While other players have tried out Aehr's systems, none has made anywhere close to the same kind of commitment.

The addition of Incal and Sonoma does at least give Aehr another product to market (at the risk of potentially cannibalizing the legacy WLTBI business). But there, too, the question of whether package-level test and burn-in will be the industry standard still remains.

And so Erickson is absolutely right: Aehr still has a lot to prove. Right now, investors are focused on the massive opportunity ahead, and excited that Aehr is showing signs it can (finally) capitalize on that opportunity. The Q3 release (and, importantly, the earnings call) need to keep that excitement going. Given how fast AEHR has raced into the release, the market has set a very high bar.

s

data.title: How Stocks Might React After A Red-Hot March CPI Report
data.description:
On April 10, 2026, the Bureau of Labor Statistics will publish the Consumer Price Index for March. In February, the CPI increased 0.3% (seasonally adjusted), after it increased by 0.2% in January. A linear pattern would suggest that CPI in March increased 0.4%. Investors consider the median CPI and the longer-term trend in the last decade first.

February Median CPI

The Cleveland Fed calculated the February median CPI at 2.85%, compared to 2.41% in the last 12 months. The median CPI calculation is the one-month inflation rate of the component where the expenditure weight is in the 50th percentile of price changes. The rationale for omitting the outliers allows the reader to focus on the interior of the distribution of price changes.

The chart below shows a smoother long-term trend. The median CPI fell steadily since 2023, while the CPI (green line) exhibited more volatility, especially between 2024 and 2025. Despite the wider monthly changes, the CPI trended lower during that time.

Oil Price Shock in March

The bar chart below shows that the one-month percent change in CPI barely changed. CPI rose by either 0.2% or 0.3%. On one occasion last May 2025, CPI increased 0.1%.

In March, the BLS data will report the impact of surging oil prices. WTI crude (CL1:COM) rose from the low $70s. It stayed at elevated levels in the $90 - $98 range for most of the month.

The index for energy already increased 0.6% in February, compared to a fall of 1.5% in January. This figure might increase to 0.6% - 0.9%. Inflation in shelter, food, medical care, and apparel might lead to an overall increase in CPI. The CPI for the last 12 months might top 3.0%. Stock markets already priced in a 97.4% chance that the Federal Reserve will hold interest rates at 350 – 375 basis points.

The odds of a rate cut to 325-350 bps were higher on February 27 at 23.3%. Not only is a rate cut probability at 0%, but the odds of a rate hike are 2.6% at the time of writing this article.

The long-term treasury bill ETF (TLT) fell by 5% in the last month.

Investors should consider buying short-term treasury bills like the 0-3 Month (SGOV) for a 3.99% yield. iShares Treasury Floating Rate Bond ETF (TFLO) offers a higher yield at 4.05%. Capital loss risks from T-bills in the 1-3 year (SHY) or the 7-10 year maturity (IEF) are minimal. They dropped by 0-51% and 0.34% in the last year, respectively.

Avoid Retail Stocks

Oil prices are not likely to fall below $90/bbl in the next three months. The shutdown of the Strait of Hormuz and damage to energy facilities in the Middle East will keep energy prices higher for longer. Consumers now pay more than $4.00 per gallon, a price not seen in around four years. Lower disposable income will hurt demand for goods that consumers do not “must have.”

Nike (NKE) is an example of an apparel supplier suffering from weak sales and high inventory levels. Although weak sales in China and changes in consumer behavior are company-specific issues, the weak outlook sent NKE stock down by 15.51% on Wednesday.

Investors should also avoid Nike’s peers, including Lululemon (LULU), On Holdings (ONON), and Deckers Outdoor (DECK). In the quant ratings below, ONON stock has a Seeking Alpha “Sell” rating. It also scores the worst in the industry rank. Nike is ranked fourth, but the momentum scores of D+ indicate a low chance of a stock rebound.

Ralph Lauren (RL) scores an A- in profitability, momentum, and EPS revisions. RL stock is worth adding to the watch list.

Automotive companies will likely face a slump in sales. Ford (F), General Motors (G), Rivian (RIVN), Stellantis (STLA), and Tesla (TSLA) lost money for investors in 2026.

Ford and GM shares have a strong buy rating.

The two American automakers scored an A+ on profitability:

The index for medical care rose 0.5% in the last year. The aging U.S. population will increase demand for medical services. Unfortunately, medical care firms like Medical Properties Trust (MPT) are not attractive holdings. AH Realty Trust (AHRT) and Chiron Real Estate (XRN) are its peers. All three firms had negative returns YTD.

Food Inflation

Higher energy costs will increase operating expenditures for food manufacturers. Investors should continue to hold an overweight position in consumer staples companies. In particular, PepsiCo (PEP), Coca-Cola (KO), and the Procter & Gamble Co. (PG) have strong branding power. Shares in those firms are positive YTD:

Above: Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP) lost around 8%, while Celsius (CELH) fell by 25%. Discount retail firms are appealing to struggling consumers. Yet Dollar Tree (DLTR) shares fell by 11.6%. The quant factor grades are red (a warning) for PG, DLTR, and PEP shares. Coke stock edges ahead of Pepsi, scoring an A+ on growth.

Your Takeaway

Stock markets have already fallen sharply since peaking in early February, before the Iran invasion. The Nasdaq (QQQ), Dow Jones (DJI), and S&P 500 (SPY) neared correction territory (-10%) before staging a rally. QQQ stock, for example, gained over 3% on Tuesday.

The U.S. might signal a desire to end its conflict in Iran. However, Iran has firm control of the Strait of Hormuz. This will likely raise oil prices, where $90/bbl is the bottom and $100 - $120/bbl is the average.

Markets likely priced in a rise in the March CPI. It would limit the upside in the indices, especially the Russell 2000 (IWM). Small-cap firms typically struggle more amid a weaker economy.

data.title: Kura Oncology: Commercialization In AML Presents Asymmetric Upside
data.description:
In December 2024, I upgraded shares of Kura Oncology (KURA) to Strong Buy ahead of the company's FDA approval of Komzifti (ziftomenib). Then as now, Kura's commercialization remains approximately 6 months behind Syndax Pharma (SNDX). While I have advocated a basket approach, holding both stocks; the investment thesis for Kura is that its differentiated safety profile, avoiding a boxed warning for QTc prolongation, positions Kura to win market share in the large front-line Acute Myeloid Leukemia (1L AML) market in patients with NPM1 mutated or KMT2A-rearranged cancer. This approval remains years in the future, with the current FDA approval representing the smaller market of R/R NPM1-m AML.

Despite this $7B opportunity, Kura has lagged the overall market, returning -8% since I first upgraded around $9.12/share. With shares currently trading in the $8-range, the market is rewarding Syndax's early lead and doubting whether Kura's putative advantage will be sufficient to win market share. Kura's market cap of ~$715M remains deeply discounted, with a minuscule enterprise value when subtracting the company's strong liquidity of $667M in cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments. The company has a partnership with the Japanese firm Kyowa Kirin, wherein profits from the drug are split and Kyowa Kirin shoulders most of the costs and pays Kura royalties. This agreement caps upside, but puts Kura in a favorable financial position, booking 100% of US sales as revenue.

Last month, Kura reported Q4 2025 earnings booking $2.1M in sales for Komzifti in only the first 5 weeks following product launch. Thus, 2026 is an execution year: to outperform the stock will have to prove the market's pessimism overdone. However, analyst's estimates for 2026 revenue with the consensus at $111.4M seems very achievable. Importantly, Kura guided for 2026 milestone payments of $45M-$55M, thus the mid-point implies $61.4M in Komzifti sales, a run rate of $15.35M per quarter or about 2.8x the very early run rate for sales in the first 5 weeks.

In this article, I will break down why current expectations seem very achievable. In addition, Kura trades at such a discount relative to its cash position that I believe it presents asymmetric upside. If expectations are too high, the company's cash balance presents investors with a substantial margin of safety; however, if the company out-performs expectations there is significant upside to the average analyst price target of $27.75/share.

Therefore, I reiterate my Strong Buy rating for shares of Kura Oncology.

Kura Oncology: Q4 2025 Earnings Update

For the fourth quarter of 2025, Kura reported Komzifti sales of $2.1 million, representing the entirety of the company's net product sales. While this is a modest amount, it was only 5 weeks into the commercialization and triggered a massive $135 million milestone payment from Kyowa Kirin. Importantly, Syndax's Revuforj is approved in R/R AML with both NPM1 mutated or KMT2A-rearranged cancers, while Kura is approved in NPM1-m R/R AML only.

Syndax identified the R/R AML KMT2A patient population as representing ~2,000 patients annually, while NPM1-m patients are ~4,500 annually. Syndax's Revuforj was approved later for NPM1-m patients on October, 24th 2025. Thus, Kura's approval in NPM1-m only lagged Syndax by approximately 3 weeks, coming on November 13th.

On the earnings call, Kura provided qualitative updates from the commercial launch, noting that within 90 days of approval 84% of private payers had established coverage aligned with the label without restrictions. This was described as: "surpass[ing] both industry benchmarks and our internal expectations."

Therefore, the competition for patients in NPM1-m R/R AML may be closer than the market believes. An optimistic trajectory for Komzifti is that it may equal the launch velocity of Revuforj, if Syndax and Kura split the larger patient population in R/R NPM1-m AML equally. The table below shows quarterly sales for Revuforj since its initial FDA approval on November 15th 2024.

Total in 2025 $124.8M --
Syndax Sales by Quarter Revuforj Net Revenue Launch Context
Q4 2024 $7.7M Initial 5 weeks of US launch
Q1 2025 $20.0M First Full Quarter of Sales
Q2 2025 $28.6M 43% QoQ Growth
Q3 2025 $32.0M 12% QoQ Growth
Q4 2025 $44.2M 38% QoQ Growth - NPM1-m Patient Population Unlocked

*Data from Syndax Press Releases. Q4 2025 saw the launch in R/R NPM1-m AML, fueling additional growth.

In order to meet expectations in 2026, Kura needs a launch that is roughly half the magnitude of Syndax's launch to achieve sales of $61.4M. Hypothetically, if Kura and Syndax split 4,500 NPM1-m patients equally they would each realize additional sales of ~$120M assuming equivalent market penetration. Therefore, the market is presently assuming that Syndax will outsell Kura by roughly 3:1.

NPM1-m and KMT2A-r patients account for roughly 50% of the available market in AML, or ~11,000 of the 22,010 cases recorded in the US each year. One reason to remain bullish here is that the overall market is so large.

Kura's management believes that R/R NPM1-m AML has a total addressable market of $350M to $400M in the US. At the mid-point ($375M), this implies a market penetration of 16.4% in the first year.

Syndax's market penetration in the first year of launch in ~2,000 R/R AML KMT2A rearranged patients was very high, reaching $88.3M in sales among these 2,000 patients. This shows how fast the adoption of menin inhibitors has been as the new standard of care in AML.

The lion's share of the AML market potential resides in 1L AML. Both Syndax and Kura are recruiting the pivotal Phase 3 trials, for Kura these are KOMET-017-NIC and IC. In 1,300 patients, these two trials will support the potential approval of Komzifti, requiring at least 2 years.

Kura's core commercial moat was broken down into four pillars: efficacy, compatibility, simplicity and safety. Primarily, Komzifti differentiates itself through safety and simplicity.

In these areas, Komzifti is the first and only once-daily oral option, boasting a significantly cleaner safety profile that entirely avoids the Boxed Warning for QTc prolongation that burdens Syndax. Further, a lack of clinically meaningful drug-drug interactions makes it compatible with patients who may be on multiple drugs simultaneously.

Ultimately, this blend of safety and simplicity should lower the barrier to near-term prescriber adoption in the R/R setting, while also positioning Komzifti as to capture share in the massive frontline market.

Kura Oncology: Analyst Consensus Expectations in 2026 and Beyond

Looking ahead, management estimates the initial US market for NPM1-m R/R AML to be $350M to $400M annually. As discussed, the current analyst consensus for Kura's 2026 total revenue sits at $111.4M.

Because Kura provided guidance for 2026 collaboration revenue of $45M to $55M, the commercial burden is significantly lower. Using the $50M midpoint, Kura needs to generate $61.4 million in net product sales this year to meet analyst expectations. Against a TAM midpoint of $375 million, Kura only needs to secure roughly 16% market penetration in its first full launch year.

If Kura can accomplish this and continue on to achieve $1.23B in sales by 2030 the earnings power will be immense. Even with profits split by the collaboration, analysts see nearly $5/share in earnings for a stock currently trading at $8/share. Clearly, if the consensus is correct shares will need to re-rate to a far higher price.

As commercialization progresses, the narrative will expand from R/R market share to combinations. Relapse rates in AML remain high, with up to 70% relapsing within 3 years. Thus, oncologists rely heavily on combination regimens, and Kura is aggressively positioning Komzifti as a foundational combination partner. The company expects to present updated intensive chemotherapy data from the KOMET-007 trial in the first half of 2026. Furthermore, because FLT3 co-mutations represent a significant portion of the AML population, Kura is actively evaluating Komzifti in combination with the FLT3 inhibitors gilteritinib in the relapsed/refractory setting and quizartinib in the frontline setting.

Because it is impossible to know in advance how this will play out, I prefer to have basket exposure to both Kura and Syndax, even if Syndax has been anointed the market leader based on stock price.

Conclusion

While investors could look at the massive revenue realized by Syndax as evidence that Kura has lost the menin inhibitor race, the reality is that the race has only just begun. As yet, investors only have a glimpse of how this will unfold based on 5 weeks of sales achieved in Q4 2025. In addition, Syndax's expansion to R/R NPM1-m AML only beat Kura to market by the slimmest of margins.

Kura is armed with a differentiated, once-daily drug that explicitly avoids the severe dual-black-box cardiac warnings of its competitor. Furthermore, with nearly $670 million in cash shielding its sub-$725 million market cap, the enterprise value of the company is barely above zero. Wall Street has set a 2026 revenue target that requires Kura to capture just 16% of the addressable market, which may prove a low hurdle for a differentiated asset with access to a deep-pocketed partner in Kyowa Kirin.

At current levels, Kura offers asymmetric upside. If the commercial launch stumbles, the massive cash position provides a substantial margin of safety. But if Kura captures even a modest slice of the market, the stock is primed for a significant re-rating toward the analyst average price target of $27.75 in the coming years.

Therefore, I reiterate my Strong Buy rating.

data.title: Booking Holdings: The Reverse Split Creates Opportunity
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

Many investors fear the term "reverse split."

Usually, a reverse split occurs more often in the fund space or with smaller companies when the share value of the company has declined so much that the company must consolidate the shares outstanding to boost per-share prices. The value of the company itself does not change, just the share price per share. If you imagine a pie, each slice of the pie gets bigger, even though the total pie itself remains the same.

For some companies, however, the value of each individual share can become so large that it causes many investors to no longer be interested in buying shares of the company. Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)(BRK.B) is famous for having exceptionally expensive individual shares. Their Class A shares trade hands for over $700,000, which effectively locks out the vast majority of retail investors from owning multiple shares. Some companies, when their share price becomes large enough that they feel that it is pricing out investors they would like to have access to their shares, will enact the opposite of a reverse split. They will enact a share split. This will turn one share into multiple shares. The same story with the pie exists. The pie remains the same size. It's cut into more slices. The idea is that more individual retail investors can enjoy a piece of the pie because it's no longer too expensive.

Today, I want to take a look at a company that is excellent in many regards, but its share price has kept common retail investors out of share ownership. That changes today with a 25-for-1 split that just occurred.

Let's dive in!

An Ecosystem That Supports Long-Term Compounding

With a supply chain of more than 4 million accommodation properties in 220+ countries and territories, Booking Holdings (BKNG) is the largest online travel agency in the world. It's known for leading brands, such as the eponymous Booking.com, Priceline, Kayak, Agoda, and OpenTable.

As we highlighted in our January article, we believe that BKNG can keep growing at a healthy clip in the years ahead. That's because it operates as the leader in a total global travel market that's worth over $3 trillion. This includes a nearly $1 trillion accommodations market, a $900 billion food and beverage market, a $750 billion air market, a $500 billion ground transportation market, and an attractions market worth $300 billion.

In the new economy, travel, experiences, and making memories are viewed by most consumers as more impactful than material possessions. A Euromonitor report released earlier this month found that the shift toward experiences is emerging as a structural change in the global economy. One in two (50%) of global consumers now state they would rather spend money on experiences than on physical possessions. As a result, many consumers are increasingly prioritizing travel over material goods.

BKNG's Q4 2025 results, reported on February 18th, provide tangible proof of this structural shift. Even with a maturing travel market, the company's total revenue surged 16% higher year-over-year (11% on a constant currency basis) to $6.35 billion in the quarter. That beat Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus during the quarter by $216 million.

Travel showed no signs of a slowdown to close out 2025, with travelers booking 285 million room nights in Q4. This 9% increase in room nights over the year-ago period even exceeded management's high-end guidance for the quarter.

Better yet, consumers overall spent more per trip, with gross bookings rising 16% (11% in constant currency) to $43 billion in the fourth quarter. This is largely due to BKNG's Connected Trip strategy (this part of the business grew at a high-20% clip in 2025, and made up a low double-digit percentage of total transactions), which is helping it to capture a larger slice of the $3 trillion global travel ecosystem.

In recent years, the company has prioritized scaling its flight vertical. That's because booking flights is generally the first thing that travelers book. This then allows BKNG to attach high-margin hotel and car rental cross-sells.

The company's adjusted EPS climbed 17.4% higher year-over-year to $48.80 during the fourth quarter. This topped the analyst consensus for the quarter by $0.13. That was driven by a combination of a relatively stable non-GAAP profit margin (24.8%) and a 2.9% reduction in BKNG's weighted average diluted share count in the quarter.

As the company continues to scale its Connected Trip business, this should allow incremental expansion in its profit margins. What's more, BKNG's CFO Ewout Steenbergen noted in his opening remarks during the Q4 2025 Earnings Call that the company anticipates delivering in-year savings of $500 million to $550 million in 2026 via its Transformation Program.

BKNG is also a financially sound enterprise, with an A- S&P credit rating and a stable outlook. Along with its strong free cash flow, this provides it with the flexibility to continue significant share repurchases. For these reasons, the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for adjusted EPS to compound by 17.1% annually through 2028, off a 2025 base of $228.06.

BKNG's 0.9% dividend yield offers modest starting income. What's most important, though, is that the company has plenty of room to hike the payout further. This is evidenced by the fact that BKNG's adjusted EPS payout ratio is expected to be in the mid-teens in 2026. That should allow it to build on its 2-year dividend growth streak with 10%+ annual dividend growth in the years ahead (for a Chowder number of roughly 11).

Also of note, BKNG's 25:1 stock split approved by the board alongside its Q4 results is set to take effect today. For those who like to avoid fractional shares, this will make it easier to buy whole shares.

From the current $4210.32 share price (as of April 1st, 2026), the stock is trading at a forward 12-month PE ratio of 15.72. For more context, this is well under the 10-year average PE ratio of 25.9, per FAST Graphs.

Moving forward, we believe that a rational fair value PE ratio for BKNG is 22. This would be a bit more than a standard deviation below the 10-year average multiple.

Fundamentally, BKNG's growth profile is as strong now as it has been in recent years. The company's 17.1% annual adjusted EPS growth consensus is higher than the 10-year CAGR of 14.6%.

Thus, our fair value multiple is conservative in that it's already pricing in mild to moderate AI disruption. That signals BKNG is the epitome of an asymmetric risk-reward slant, with shares priced 27% below our fair value per share estimate of $6,089.

If it returns to fair value and matches growth forecasts, the stock could post a 64% total return by the end of 2027.

Now, BKNG does face risks that could set back its investment thesis. These include the long-term risk of margin pressures from the likes of Alphabet (GOOGL)(GOOG) and rising recession risk.

Last November, Google announced that it was rolling out AI Mode and Canvas planning tools. These features allow travelers to generate full itineraries and search for real-time flight deals in 200 countries. What's more, the company has introduced agentic capabilities to enable Gemini to complete tasks, such as comparing hotel amenities and initiating reservations.

While the risk of Google bypassing BKNG altogether is low (Google has repeatedly stated it has no intention of becoming an OTA, and it needs BKNG's huge backend infrastructure and global supply chain to fulfill requests), there is a risk to the latter's margins. That's because Gemini is fundamentally transforming how traffic is sent to BKNG.

Whereas the old funnel was a Google search, clicking on a Booking.com ad, and searching/booking on Booking.com, the new funnel is likely to be talking to Gemini, it finds a room through BKNG's API, the user confirms in the chat, and BKNG processes the payment in the background. In such cases, BKNG loses its front-door brand identity. That could eventually prompt Google to charge more for it at any time, which would at least somewhat crimp profit margins.

In February, Marriott International (MAR) became the first major hotel to recently launch a direct-booking integration with Google's AI mode. This allows users to skip the commissions that they would typically pay to BKNG.

Still, the mitigation factor is that millions of independent apartments, boutique hotels, and villas that comprise most of BKNG's high-margin inventory are too fragmented to do so, which is why Google needs a player like BKNG to access this part of the market.

Another risk to the company is the potential for surging crude oil prices to pressure the discretionary income of consumers at the grocery store and at the pump. Since the U.S. and Israel began strikes on Iran, Kalshi's betting odds for a U.S. recession have jumped from 21.6% to 32.5%. Even if this doesn't come to pass, travel demand will likely be reduced in the near term.

Conclusion

BKNG is a company that is focused on helping you enjoy your travel. They do so by enabling countless merchants offering their wares to be connected to millions of consumers looking for exactly those products. Because it does the work of a middleman, it doesn't need a lot of assets. It's not owning the hotels, it doesn't own the airlines, and it doesn't own the experiences. It simply offers the opportunity to connect those two together for you. This exposes them to a unique set of opportunities and risks. However, BKNG is well-positioned to provide the infrastructure needed for continued travel reservations from different access points.

BKNG is focused on growing its dividends and actively buying back its shares to reward shareholders. The recent stock split enables more shares to be available at lower overall prices, but the value of the company and its value proposition remains the same. Furthermore, it enables the company to continue to buy back shares from investors who may want to trim a position but didn't want to sell a $4000 piece of their portfolio all at once. The goal of a stock split is to create more volume and opportunities for additional investors to buy into the company. Often, a stock split can help a company's overall value rise more rapidly because it enables more interest from more parties. At the end of the day, my portfolio is looking for strong total returns, both from growing dividends and from climbing share values. I don't have an interest in buying a company that is not going to provide me with strong total returns over the long run. I am a long-term buy-and-hold style investor looking for total returns over the long run and am willing to accept sell-offs because of long-term return expectations in the end. This is the classic type of holding for that scenario for a true Dividend King-style investor.

Ignore the chaos of sentiment; find clarity.

data.title: Veralto: Quality Play Is Gradually Winning Me Over
data.description:
Shares of Veralto (VLTO) have fallen quite a bit in the first quarter, despite a resilient end to 2025. The company continues to perform a balancing act between organic growth, M&A, and buybacks, which provide an upward lift to the fundamental performance. This, in combination with falling prices, makes earnings multiples rapidly come down, with appeal being on the increase here.

Frankly, I like the moves made by Veralto, as recent bolt-on dealmaking comes at a price. While shares have come down quite a bit, I like the lower expectations, but it requires a slightly larger pullback before pulling the trigger here to get involved.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent M&A efforts, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

A Bolt-On Deal To End The Quarter

On the final day of the first quarter, Veralto announced the acquisition of GlobalVision. This Montreal-based business leverages proprietary deterministic technology, which helps pharmaceutical and consumer packaged goods customers with speed to market and packaging compliance regulations.

These solutions will be integrated with Veralto's Esko business to meet these customers in facing ever-stringent requirements regarding time to market, consistent branding, and regulatory requirements.

The business is seen contributing $25 million in sales this year, with EBITDA margins seen around 30%, for a $7.5 million EBITDA number. However, Veralto sees EBITDA rise to $13 million; this is defined as the 2026 adjusted EBITDA plus cost synergies. These synergies are thus seen at $5.5 million, suggesting synergies exceeding 20% of sales!

With a 15 times multiple applied to a $13 million pro forma EBITDA number, the purchase price of $195 million is equal to nearly 8 times sales and 26 times standalone EBITDA, both being rather demanding multiples here. The deal is seen as having a neutral impact on 2026 earnings, with accretion seen to 2027 earnings, although this has not been quantified.

The company furthermore provided an update on the share buyback program. With 3.2 million shares bought back at a combined $300 million price tag, this suggests average purchase prices at $93 and change. This is equal to about 1.3% of the pro forma share count.

Pro Forma Implications

Early in February, Veralto announced its 2025 results, with full-year sales up 6% to $5.50 billion, as non-core sales growth came in just shy of 5%. Growth was spread pretty evenly between a $3.3 billion water quality business and a near $2.2 billion product quality & innovation business. Water quality focuses on product solutions that relate to water scarcity, contamination, and quality, while product quality & innovation is about coloring, packaging, marking, and conditioning.

Already fat non-GAAP operating margins of 24.3% of sales rose another 20 basis points on the year before, with margins reported at similar levels across both segments. Non-GAAP earnings of $977 million were equal to $3.90 per share. The reconciliation to GAAP earnings was both fair and small, with GAAP earnings reported at $3.76 per share.

Net debt is reported at $642 million, which is a very modest number in relation to adjusted operating profits of $1.34 billion. Factoring in the impact of the recent deal and $300 million in buybacks in the first quarter, I see pro forma net debt just over $1.1 billion. That, however, is not all, as the company closed on the acquisition of In-Situ in January, that $435 million deal having been announced in November of last year.

The company guided for 2026 core sales to grow by low- to mid-single digits, which, combined with modest margin expansion, should result in adjusted earnings advancing to $4.10-$4.20 per share.

Trading down to $88 per share, the 250 million shares grant Veralto a $22 billion equity valuation and a $22.7 billion enterprise valuation ahead of the recent capital allocation moves. This values the business at around 4 times sales, with equity trading around 22-23 times trailing earnings; this multiple is seen dropping a turn based on forward earnings.

Of course, the purchase of GlobalVision is only set to boost sales by half a point. However, the deal brings real accretion to margins and likely the organic growth profile of the business as well, although that is too small to make a real dent.

What Now?

I last covered shares of Veralto on the first day of the year, concluding that Veralto was putting some capital to work with the purchase of In-Situ. This was accompanied by the announcement of a $750 million buyback program, 40% of which was completed in the first quarter.

Between that buyback program and two deals closing in the first quarter, pro forma net debt of $1.5 billion is largely in line with EBITDA. Leverage is far from a concern, while a more than 10% pullback to $88 per share makes it so that earnings multiples have fallen by 2-3 times. However, the company first had to deliver on all this, as both bolt-on deals are anticipated to see aggressive synergy targets. These first have to be delivered upon to justify their deals in isolation, but in the grand scheme of things, these impacts are relatively small.

In between shares pulling back and earnings coming in a bit stronger than expected, multiples are coming in. Further growth is seen in 2026, as mid-twenty times earnings multiples have fallen to the low-twenties, which is compelling. Shares are now trading at the lowest levels since the spring of 2024 following the separation from Danaher, as valuation multiples have gradually come down.

This makes me more upbeat here, in between a modest premium valuation multiple applied, moderate leverage employed, and Veralto finding a good balance in between organic growth, a strong positioning, buybacks, and M&A efforts.

This makes shares of the company, which aims to safeguard the world's vital resources, rapidly become a safer place for investors as well. With M&A efforts relying on aggressive synergy targets, and elevated valuation multiples, I am waiting for a slightly larger pullback to the low eighties before gradually buying the dip here.
